[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|

File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|

Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|

File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] [[Category:Dozen Islands]] [[Category:Kagura]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:SuQqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-Dozen Kagura}}

The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|

[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine.
