[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|

revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] [[Category:Dozen Islands]] [[Category:Kagura]] [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-Dozen Kagura}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-Dozen Kagura}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=draft}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=draft}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=draft}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=draft}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島|12=draft}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島|12=draft}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町|12=draft}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|12=draft}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|12=draft}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=draft}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社|12=draft}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=draft}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後|12=draft}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=draft}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家|12=draft}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職|12=draft}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=draft}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|12=draft}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂|12=draft}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=draft}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=draft}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=draft}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=draft}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=draft}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=draft}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{draft categories| [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] [[Category:Dozen Islands]] [[Category:Kagura]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[en:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-Dozen Kagura}}

However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=draft}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家|12=draft}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職|12=draft}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=draft}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|12=draft}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂|12=draft}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=draft}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=draft}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=draft}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=draft}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=draft}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|

[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島|12=draft}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島|12=draft}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町|12=draft}}). [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島|12=draft}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島|12=draft}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=draft}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町|12=draft}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|12=draft}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|12=draft}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=draft}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社|12=draft}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=draft}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後|12=draft}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=draft}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家|12=draft}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職|12=draft}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=draft}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|12=draft}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂|12=draft}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=draft}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=draft}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=draft}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=draft}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=draft}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=draft}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{draft categories| [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] [[Category:Dozen Islands]] [[Category:Kagura]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[en:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-Dozen Kagura}}

The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] [[Category:Dozen Islands]] [[Category:Kagura]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[en:Oki-Dozen Kagura]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-Dozen Kagura}}

revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家|1=Shake (social class)|2=ja|3=社家|4=id|5=Shake (kelas sosial)|comment=Is there an actual longstanding pun comparing this to the Shakya clan of Siddartha or is that just chatGPT being wonky|lt=Shrine family}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

revision.comment: 15 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:隠岐島前神楽 up to rev 92463223
revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|由良比女神社で行われる隠岐島前神楽]] '''隠岐島前神楽'''（おきどうぜんかぐら）は、[[島根県]][[隠岐諸島]]の[[島前|島前3島]]で伝承されている[[神楽]]である。島根県の[[無形民俗文化財]]。 == 概要 == 島前3島は[[西ノ島]]（[[西ノ島町]]）、[[中ノ島 (島根県)|中ノ島]]（[[海士町]]）、[[知夫里島]]（[[知夫村]]）から成り、各集落の[[神社]]において奉納される。かつては[[祈雨]]や病気平癒といった[[祈祷]]を目的とする「大注連神楽（おおしめかぐら）」、神社では[[遷座祭]]での「湯立大神楽（ゆだてだいかぐら）」と平素の祭礼における「大神楽」、豊漁祈願の際の「浜神楽（はまかぐら）」と、目的や行事の大きさによって類別されていたが、現在では夏祭りでの「大神楽」のみが行われている。 毎年行われる西ノ島町美田の[[焼火神社]]（7月23日）のものが著名であるが、その他同町浦郷の[[由良比女神社]]における海上渡御祭（隔年の7月下旬）、同町船越の高田神社（隔年7月18日）、同町別府の[[海神社 (西ノ島町)|海神社]]（隔年7月21日）などがあり、特に由良比女神社のものは船上で舞われる点が珍しい三上敏視「隠岐島前神楽」（別冊太陽『お神楽』所収）。。島前3島とともに隠岐諸島を成す[[島後島]]に伝承される[[島後神楽]]と合わせて「隠岐神楽」と呼ばれていた事もあり、[[巫女]]が重要な役割を果たしたり、ほぼ同様の演目を持つなど共通する点も多いが、島後神楽の悠長な[[囃子]]に対して、速めで賑やかな囃子であることや島根県立古代出雲歴史博物館、[http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html 島前神楽]（平成21年7月20日閲覧）。、同じ演目でも内容が異なるなどの相異を見せ、現在では「島前神楽」「島後神楽」と別々に呼ばれている。この島前神楽は、（1）巫女による[[神懸り]]の形が保存されている、（2）[[出雲神楽]]の祖形が遺存すると思われる点があり、古態のままが保存されている、（3）演戯のみでなく神事の要素も保存されている、という事由で、[[昭和]]36年（[[1961年]]）6月13日に島根県の無形民俗文化財に指定された『島前の文化財』第1号。。 == 伝承 == 隠岐諸島には古来、特定の[[神社]]に属さず、祈祷のための神楽を専業とする'''社家'''（しゃけ）と呼ばれる神楽師がおり、島前には5家の社家筋が神楽を伝えたが浦郷の秋月家、別府の宇野家、布施の秋月家、知夫の石塚家、海士の駒月家（『島前の文化財』第1号）。、現在では秋月・石塚の2家のみが残り、この両家に一般人を加えて組織された隠岐島前神楽保存会で伝承しているほか、各地で[[同好会]]が結成されて伝承に努めている。神楽社家は[[神職]]と神楽師の中間的な形態で、集落の[[氏神]]の祭礼神楽や祈雨や漁業における祈祷神楽を掌った隠岐独特の制度である。この社家の起源は詳らかでないが、島前における幣頭（へいとう。社家筋の筆頭）であった秋月家の家伝によると、その先祖は[[藤原氏]]に連なる者で、[[天文 (日本)|天文年間]]（16世紀中葉）以前に隠岐に土着、吉田姓を名乗って来たといい『日本の祭り文化事典』。、社家は明治までは[[京都]]の[[吉田家]]から神楽斎行の許状を得ており、そのため[[吉田神道]]の[[行法]]が組み込まれたと推定される要素も持つ。 == 神楽場 == [[神楽殿]]などの常設の施設はなく、演舞に際しては仮設の舞台が設置される。舞台は2[[間]]四方の8畳間を基本とするが、その中の方1間（2畳敷分）の範囲で舞う点が特徴である。神楽場の中央上方には[[天井]]から「玉蓋（ぎょくがい）」と称す[[天蓋]]を吊し、その玉蓋には5色（青、黄、赤、白、紫）の[[紙垂]]を垂らすとともに、四方に[[大幣|大幣（おおぬさ）]]を張り出し、これらを「雲」と称す。また舞場の後方中央に幕を垂らしてそこから演者が出入りし、3方には楽人が座して囃子を担当する。 == 奏楽 == 囃子手は上述のように舞場を3方から囲み、正面奥の向かって右から「胴（胴長）」と称する[[長胴太鼓]]1人と「太鼓」（[[締太鼓]]）3人の計4人が、左右側に「手拍子」と称する銅拍子（[[手平鉦|手平鉦（てひらがね）]]）を担当する者2人ずつが座す。[[笛]]はなく、「胴」が始終[[謡曲]]のような「神歌」を歌うのが特徴である。また、演奏は4分の4拍子と4分の3拍子が交互に繰り返されるが、[[日本]]の伝統音楽において4分の3拍子というのは珍しく、一方で[[朝鮮]]の伝統音楽には多いために[[朝鮮半島]]との関係を考える上でも注目される。 == 構成 == 大きく[[直面|直面（ひためん）]]で舞われる「舞い」と、[[神楽面]]を着した「能」とに大別される。「舞い」には神楽場を清め神々を招く（神招（かみお）ぎ）「前座の舞」や、[[巫女]]による「神子（みこ）神楽」があり、「能」には「式三番の能」と「式外（しきがい/しきげ）の能」の2種がある。「能」は[[出雲]]地方の沖合に位置するという地理条件から、いわゆる[[出雲神話]]を題材とする[[出雲神楽]]の影響が指摘でき、特に演目などには[[佐陀神能]]の影響がうかがえる。また、今でこそ見られないが、「式外の能」の中に[[葬祭]]において舞われるものがあった点も特徴で、現在の「八重垣（やえがき）」はそれを改作したものであるという焼火神社、[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html 島前神楽]（平成21年7月20日閲覧）。。なお、かつては夜を徹して演じられ、明け方に巫女が「注連行事（しめぎょうじ）」を舞い、神楽の最重要な神懸りによる[[託宣]]が行われたが、明治になって神懸りが禁止されたため、神子神楽にかすかに形式を残すのみとなった。 == 演目 == （以下、年度や場所により出入りがあるため、平成3年（1991年）の例をもとに記述する。）『日本の祭り文化事典』参照。但し、どこで行われたものかは記していないため不明。また各演目については、焼火神社、[https://takuhi.hatenadiary.org/entries/2008/07/30 たくひブログ（隠岐島前神楽番組説明）]（平成21年7月20日閲覧）も参照した。 「集まり給へ、四方（よも）の神々」と歌う「寄せ楽（よせがく）」で神楽が開始される。 === 前座の舞 === 直面の儀式舞。祭場を大幣などで清め、[[神々]]を招く。 *神途舞（かんどまい） - 1人舞で、左手に紙垂を垂らした[[榊]]、右手に[[中啓]]を採り、「ここは[[高天原|高天原（たかまがはら）]]なれば」という神歌に合わせて東西南北と天地の6方向を清める。 *入申（いりもうし） - [[奉幣]]の行事。 *御座清め・神子舞 - 1人舞。[[天冠]]を被り、白の[[小袖]]に[[千早]]、[[緋袴]]を着した巫女が、[[鈴]]を左手に採り、右手で袖を取りながら四方に舞い、最後に右回り（反時計回り）、左回り（時計回り）と激しく旋回する舞いとなる。神招ぎの舞いである。 *散供（さんぐう） - 「幣舞（ぬさまい）」ともいう1人舞。鈴と大幣を採り、鈴を振りながら神楽の由来と趣旨を述べ、その後[[稲]]を撒いて6方を拝む。 === 式三番の能 === 必須とされる儀式的な3番の能。 *先払い - 1人舞。[[猿田彦大神]]の面を被り、長さ2[[尺]]程度の[[竹]]の両端に紙を巻き付けた「打杖（うちづえ）」を[[採物]]に、[[天孫降臨]]に先だって邪霊を払う所作をする。 *湯立（ゆだて） - 最初に巫女姿の「熊野の巫女」が現れ、次いで「御祖神（みおやがみ）」が出て来て榊を振り回し、「熊野の巫女」と問答の末、激しく舞った後に湯に手を入れて湯を浴びる所作をする。 *随神（ずいじん） - 「八幡（はちまん）」ともいう2人舞。白面で左手に[[弓 (武器)|弓]]、右手に[[矢]]を採った「豊間戸奇間戸神（とよまどくしまどのかみ）」と2本の[[角]]を持つ黒い[[鬼面]]を被った邪神が問答の後、矢で射られた邪神が退散する。 === 式外の能 === *切部（きりべ） - 「市切部（いちきりべ）」ともいう。最初に[[木花佐久夜姫|木花佐久夜姫（このはなさくやひめ）]]が登場して舞い、次いで[[武甕槌神|建雷之神（たけいかずちのかみ）]]が登場、木花佐久夜姫は退場して、建雷之神が長胴太鼓を打ちながら戦の有様を語る。胴太鼓の[[曲打ち]]が見られる能であるため、盛り上がりを見せる演目でもある。 *恵比須（えびす） - 当地では地理環境から漁業が盛んで、そこから漁業を司る[[恵比須神]]に対する信仰（特に[[美保神社]]への信仰）も起こり、信仰の対象である恵比須神を主役とする豊漁祈願の能として舞われている。恵比須神が「打杖」を竿に見立てて[[鯛]]を釣る所作を舞い、その後採物を扇に持ち替えて喜びの舞いを舞う。見物人との掛け合いが見られる点も島前神楽では珍しいものとなっている。 *八重垣 - [[素戔嗚尊]]の[[八岐大蛇|八衢の大蛇（やまたのおろち）]]退治に因んだ能。初めに榊を採物とした[[奇稲田姫]]と扇を採物とした翁（[[脚摩乳|足名槌]]）が登場し、次いで「打杖」を採った素戔嗚尊が登場、素戔嗚尊と翁の掛け合いの後に紙垂をつけた榊を両手に採った八衢の大蛇が登場、素戔嗚尊に退治される。大蛇は[[蛇]]ではなく[[トカゲ|蜥蜴]]の容姿で、幕から出ずに中腰のまま舞う点が島前神楽の特徴である。 最後に神楽場に集まった神々を還す（[[昇神]]）「神戻し（かんもどし）」が奏されて神楽を終える。 === その他 === *舞い児（まいこ） - 神子神楽の1つ。神子神楽には島前神楽の演目として舞われる「本格式」と、それを簡略化して神社の祭典で舞うもの、そしてこの「舞い児」の3種があり、「舞い児」は「本格式」と神社の祭典の両者で適宜舞われている。巫女が1歳未満の幼児を抱き、その無事な成長を祈願するものであるが、「本格式」の場合、主として神楽が行われた翌朝の「朝神楽」で舞われる。 *式外の能には他に「鵜ノ羽」、「十羅」、「佐陀」という曲がある。 ==　ギャラリー　== 以下の写真は由良比女神社で行われた隠岐島前神楽の様子である。 File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (5).jpg|

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[隠岐島前神楽]] to [[Oki-Dozen Kagura]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|由良比女神社で行われる隠岐島前神楽]] '''隠岐島前神楽'''（おきどうぜんかぐら）は、[[島根県]][[隠岐諸島]]の[[島前|島前3島]]で伝承されている[[神楽]]である。島根県の[[無形民俗文化財]]。 == 概要 == 島前3島は[[西ノ島]]（[[西ノ島町]]）、[[中ノ島 (島根県)|中ノ島]]（[[海士町]]）、[[知夫里島]]（[[知夫村]]）から成り、各集落の[[神社]]において奉納される。かつては[[祈雨]]や病気平癒といった[[祈祷]]を目的とする「大注連神楽（おおしめかぐら）」、神社では[[遷座祭]]での「湯立大神楽（ゆだてだいかぐら）」と平素の祭礼における「大神楽」、豊漁祈願の際の「浜神楽（はまかぐら）」と、目的や行事の大きさによって類別されていたが、現在では夏祭りでの「大神楽」のみが行われている。 毎年行われる西ノ島町美田の[[焼火神社]]（7月23日）のものが著名であるが、その他同町浦郷の[[由良比女神社]]における海上渡御祭（隔年の7月下旬）、同町船越の高田神社（隔年7月18日）、同町別府の[[海神社 (西ノ島町)|海神社]]（隔年7月21日）などがあり、特に由良比女神社のものは船上で舞われる点が珍しい三上敏視「隠岐島前神楽」（別冊太陽『お神楽』所収）。。島前3島とともに隠岐諸島を成す[[島後島]]に伝承される[[島後神楽]]と合わせて「隠岐神楽」と呼ばれていた事もあり、[[巫女]]が重要な役割を果たしたり、ほぼ同様の演目を持つなど共通する点も多いが、島後神楽の悠長な[[囃子]]に対して、速めで賑やかな囃子であることや島根県立古代出雲歴史博物館、[http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html 島前神楽]（平成21年7月20日閲覧）。、同じ演目でも内容が異なるなどの相異を見せ、現在では「島前神楽」「島後神楽」と別々に呼ばれている。この島前神楽は、（1）巫女による[[神懸り]]の形が保存されている、（2）[[出雲神楽]]の祖形が遺存すると思われる点があり、古態のままが保存されている、（3）演戯のみでなく神事の要素も保存されている、という事由で、[[昭和]]36年（[[1961年]]）6月13日に島根県の無形民俗文化財に指定された『島前の文化財』第1号。。 == 伝承 == 隠岐諸島には古来、特定の[[神社]]に属さず、祈祷のための神楽を専業とする'''社家'''（しゃけ）と呼ばれる神楽師がおり、島前には5家の社家筋が神楽を伝えたが浦郷の秋月家、別府の宇野家、布施の秋月家、知夫の石塚家、海士の駒月家（『島前の文化財』第1号）。、現在では秋月・石塚の2家のみが残り、この両家に一般人を加えて組織された隠岐島前神楽保存会で伝承しているほか、各地で[[同好会]]が結成されて伝承に努めている。神楽社家は[[神職]]と神楽師の中間的な形態で、集落の[[氏神]]の祭礼神楽や祈雨や漁業における祈祷神楽を掌った隠岐独特の制度である。この社家の起源は詳らかでないが、島前における幣頭（へいとう。社家筋の筆頭）であった秋月家の家伝によると、その先祖は[[藤原氏]]に連なる者で、[[天文 (日本)|天文年間]]（16世紀中葉）以前に隠岐に土着、吉田姓を名乗って来たといい『日本の祭り文化事典』。、社家は明治までは[[京都]]の[[吉田家]]から神楽斎行の許状を得ており、そのため[[吉田神道]]の[[行法]]が組み込まれたと推定される要素も持つ。 == 神楽場 == [[神楽殿]]などの常設の施設はなく、演舞に際しては仮設の舞台が設置される。舞台は2[[間]]四方の8畳間を基本とするが、その中の方1間（2畳敷分）の範囲で舞う点が特徴である。神楽場の中央上方には[[天井]]から「玉蓋（ぎょくがい）」と称す[[天蓋]]を吊し、その玉蓋には5色（青、黄、赤、白、紫）の[[紙垂]]を垂らすとともに、四方に[[大幣|大幣（おおぬさ）]]を張り出し、これらを「雲」と称す。また舞場の後方中央に幕を垂らしてそこから演者が出入りし、3方には楽人が座して囃子を担当する。 == 奏楽 == 囃子手は上述のように舞場を3方から囲み、正面奥の向かって右から「胴（胴長）」と称する[[長胴太鼓]]1人と「太鼓」（[[締太鼓]]）3人の計4人が、左右側に「手拍子」と称する銅拍子（[[手平鉦|手平鉦（てひらがね）]]）を担当する者2人ずつが座す。[[笛]]はなく、「胴」が始終[[謡曲]]のような「神歌」を歌うのが特徴である。また、演奏は4分の4拍子と4分の3拍子が交互に繰り返されるが、[[日本]]の伝統音楽において4分の3拍子というのは珍しく、一方で[[朝鮮]]の伝統音楽には多いために[[朝鮮半島]]との関係を考える上でも注目される。 == 構成 == 大きく[[直面|直面（ひためん）]]で舞われる「舞い」と、[[神楽面]]を着した「能」とに大別される。「舞い」には神楽場を清め神々を招く（神招（かみお）ぎ）「前座の舞」や、[[巫女]]による「神子（みこ）神楽」があり、「能」には「式三番の能」と「式外（しきがい/しきげ）の能」の2種がある。「能」は[[出雲]]地方の沖合に位置するという地理条件から、いわゆる[[出雲神話]]を題材とする[[出雲神楽]]の影響が指摘でき、特に演目などには[[佐陀神能]]の影響がうかがえる。また、今でこそ見られないが、「式外の能」の中に[[葬祭]]において舞われるものがあった点も特徴で、現在の「八重垣（やえがき）」はそれを改作したものであるという焼火神社、[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html 島前神楽]（平成21年7月20日閲覧）。。なお、かつては夜を徹して演じられ、明け方に巫女が「注連行事（しめぎょうじ）」を舞い、神楽の最重要な神懸りによる[[託宣]]が行われたが、明治になって神懸りが禁止されたため、神子神楽にかすかに形式を残すのみとなった。 == 演目 == （以下、年度や場所により出入りがあるため、平成3年（1991年）の例をもとに記述する。）『日本の祭り文化事典』参照。但し、どこで行われたものかは記していないため不明。また各演目については、焼火神社、[https://takuhi.hatenadiary.org/entries/2008/07/30 たくひブログ（隠岐島前神楽番組説明）]（平成21年7月20日閲覧）も参照した。 「集まり給へ、四方（よも）の神々」と歌う「寄せ楽（よせがく）」で神楽が開始される。 === 前座の舞 === 直面の儀式舞。祭場を大幣などで清め、[[神々]]を招く。 *神途舞（かんどまい） - 1人舞で、左手に紙垂を垂らした[[榊]]、右手に[[中啓]]を採り、「ここは[[高天原|高天原（たかまがはら）]]なれば」という神歌に合わせて東西南北と天地の6方向を清める。 *入申（いりもうし） - [[奉幣]]の行事。 *御座清め・神子舞 - 1人舞。[[天冠]]を被り、白の[[小袖]]に[[千早]]、[[緋袴]]を着した巫女が、[[鈴]]を左手に採り、右手で袖を取りながら四方に舞い、最後に右回り（反時計回り）、左回り（時計回り）と激しく旋回する舞いとなる。神招ぎの舞いである。 *散供（さんぐう） - 「幣舞（ぬさまい）」ともいう1人舞。鈴と大幣を採り、鈴を振りながら神楽の由来と趣旨を述べ、その後[[稲]]を撒いて6方を拝む。 === 式三番の能 === 必須とされる儀式的な3番の能。 *先払い - 1人舞。[[猿田彦大神]]の面を被り、長さ2[[尺]]程度の[[竹]]の両端に紙を巻き付けた「打杖（うちづえ）」を[[採物]]に、[[天孫降臨]]に先だって邪霊を払う所作をする。 *湯立（ゆだて） - 最初に巫女姿の「熊野の巫女」が現れ、次いで「御祖神（みおやがみ）」が出て来て榊を振り回し、「熊野の巫女」と問答の末、激しく舞った後に湯に手を入れて湯を浴びる所作をする。 *随神（ずいじん） - 「八幡（はちまん）」ともいう2人舞。白面で左手に[[弓 (武器)|弓]]、右手に[[矢]]を採った「豊間戸奇間戸神（とよまどくしまどのかみ）」と2本の[[角]]を持つ黒い[[鬼面]]を被った邪神が問答の後、矢で射られた邪神が退散する。 === 式外の能 === *切部（きりべ） - 「市切部（いちきりべ）」ともいう。最初に[[木花佐久夜姫|木花佐久夜姫（このはなさくやひめ）]]が登場して舞い、次いで[[武甕槌神|建雷之神（たけいかずちのかみ）]]が登場、木花佐久夜姫は退場して、建雷之神が長胴太鼓を打ちながら戦の有様を語る。胴太鼓の[[曲打ち]]が見られる能であるため、盛り上がりを見せる演目でもある。 *恵比須（えびす） - 当地では地理環境から漁業が盛んで、そこから漁業を司る[[恵比須神]]に対する信仰（特に[[美保神社]]への信仰）も起こり、信仰の対象である恵比須神を主役とする豊漁祈願の能として舞われている。恵比須神が「打杖」を竿に見立てて[[鯛]]を釣る所作を舞い、その後採物を扇に持ち替えて喜びの舞いを舞う。見物人との掛け合いが見られる点も島前神楽では珍しいものとなっている。 *八重垣 - [[素戔嗚尊]]の[[八岐大蛇|八衢の大蛇（やまたのおろち）]]退治に因んだ能。初めに榊を採物とした[[奇稲田姫]]と扇を採物とした翁（[[脚摩乳|足名槌]]）が登場し、次いで「打杖」を採った素戔嗚尊が登場、素戔嗚尊と翁の掛け合いの後に紙垂をつけた榊を両手に採った八衢の大蛇が登場、素戔嗚尊に退治される。大蛇は[[蛇]]ではなく[[トカゲ|蜥蜴]]の容姿で、幕から出ずに中腰のまま舞う点が島前神楽の特徴である。 最後に神楽場に集まった神々を還す（[[昇神]]）「神戻し（かんもどし）」が奏されて神楽を終える。 === その他 === *舞い児（まいこ） - 神子神楽の1つ。神子神楽には島前神楽の演目として舞われる「本格式」と、それを簡略化して神社の祭典で舞うもの、そしてこの「舞い児」の3種があり、「舞い児」は「本格式」と神社の祭典の両者で適宜舞われている。巫女が1歳未満の幼児を抱き、その無事な成長を祈願するものであるが、「本格式」の場合、主として神楽が行われた翌朝の「朝神楽」で舞われる。 *式外の能には他に「鵜ノ羽」、「十羅」、「佐陀」という曲がある。 ==　ギャラリー　== 以下の写真は由良比女神社で行われた隠岐島前神楽の様子である。 File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|

revision.text:
[[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of {{ill|kagura|ja|神楽}} preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前}} in the {{ill|Oki Islands|ja|隠岐諸島}} of {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise {{ill|Nishinoshima|ja|西ノ島町}}, {{ill|Chiburi Island|ja|知夫里島}} ({{ill|Chibu|ja|知夫村}}), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島}} ({{ill|Ama|ja|海士町}}). The kagura is performed at various {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and {{ill|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)|ja|海神社}} in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽}} on {{ill|Dōgo Island|ja|島後}}, such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called {{ill|Shake (Shinto)|ja|社家|1=Shake (social class)|2=ja|3=社家|4=id|5=Shake (kelas sosial)|comment=Is there an actual longstanding pun comparing this to the Shakya clan of Siddartha or is that just chatGPT being wonky|lt=Shrine family}}, who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as {{ill|Shinto priests|ja|神職}} and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored {{ill|Shide|ja|紙垂}} hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine.
