revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|qq=|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|qq=|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{translated page|ja|隠岐島前神楽|version=92463223|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kagura]] }} [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[Category:Shimane Islands]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q11659047}}

However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

[[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|qq=|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|qq=|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{translated page|ja|隠岐島前神楽|version=92463223|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kagura]] }} [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[Category:Shimane Islands]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] {{wikidata link|Q11659047}}

Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. revision.text:
[[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{translated page|ja|隠岐島前神楽|version=92463223|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kagura]] }} [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[Category:Shimane Islands]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] {{wikidata link|Q11659047}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|

[[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{translated page|ja|隠岐島前神楽|version=92463223|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kagura]] }} [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[Category:Shimane Islands]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] {{wikidata link|Q11659047}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
[[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (6).jpg|thumb|250px|right|Oki-Dozen Kagura performed at Yurahime Shrine]] '''Oki-Dozen Kagura''' (隠岐島前神楽) is a form of [[:en:Kagura|kagura]] preserved on the {{ill|Dozen Islands|ja|島前|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozen Islands|1=Dōzen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476216}} in the [[:en:Oki Islands|Oki Islands]] of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by the prefecture. == Overview == The Dozen Islands comprise [[:en:Nishinoshima, Shimane|Nishinoshima]], [[:en:Chiburijima|Chiburi Island]] ([[:en:Chibu, Shimane|Chibu]]), and {{ill|Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja|中ノ島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Island (Shimane)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中之島|1=Nakanoshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6960286}} ([[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama]]). The kagura is performed at various [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] across these islands. Historically, different types of kagura were performed depending on the purpose and occasion, including "Ōshime Kagura" for {{ill|rainmaking|ja|祈雨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rainmaking|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雨乞い|1=rainmaking rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q477738}} and healing, "Yutate Daikagura" for shrine transfer ceremonies, "Daikagura" for regular festivals, and "Hama Kagura" for bountiful fishing prayers. Today, only the summer "Daikagura" remains active. The most notable annual performance occurs on July 23 at {{ill|Akahime Shrine|ja|焼火神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akahime Shrine|1=Takuhi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567668}} in Mitani, Nishinoshima. Other significant events include the maritime procession festival at {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} (late July, biennial), Takada Shrine in Funakoshi (July 18, biennial), and [[:en:Watatsumi Shrine|Kaijin Shrine (Nishinoshima)]] in Beppu (July 21, biennial). The kagura performed aboard a boat at Yurahime Shrine is particularly unique.Mikami Toshimi, "Oki-Dozen Kagura," in *Bessatsu Taiyō: Kagura*, Heibonsha. Oki-Dozen Kagura shares common elements with {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on [[:en:Dōgojima|Dōgo Island]], such as the roles of shrine maidens and similar repertoire, and was once collectively referred to as "Oki Kagura." However, distinctions include faster, more vibrant music compared to the leisurely rhythms of Oki-Kaigo Kagura. These differences have led to the modern differentiation between "Dozen Kagura" and "Kaigo Kagura." Dozen Kagura retains ancient forms, preserving elements such as (1) trance-like possession by shrine maidens, (2) prototypes of {{ill|Izumo Kagura|ja|出雲神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and (3) ritualistic significance alongside performance art. For these reasons, it was designated a prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 13, 1961.*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1. == Tradition == The Oki Islands historically hosted independent kagura practitioners called [[:en:Shake (social class)|Shrine family]], who were not affiliated with specific shrines. On the Dozen Islands, five shake families preserved the kagura tradition.The families included the Akizuki family in Uragō, Uno family in Beppu, Akizuki family in Fuse, Ishizuka family in Chibu, and Komazuki family in Ama (*Cultural Properties of the Dozen Islands*, Issue 1). Today, only the Akizuki and Ishizuka families remain. These families, along with lay members, form the Oki-Dozen Kagura Preservation Society, which transmits the tradition. Kagura clubs have also been established across the islands. The shake were unique to the Oki Islands, serving both as [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] and kagura performers for shrine rituals and fishing-related prayers. According to family lore, the Akizuki family, the leading shake on the Dozen Islands, descended from the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and settled in the islands during the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (mid-16th century). The families received permissions from the {{ill|Urabe clan|lt=Urabe clan|lt=Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|qq=}} in Kyoto to conduct kagura ceremonies, incorporating elements of [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]]. == Kagura Stage == Performances take place on temporary stages, typically 2 *ken* (approximately 3.6 meters) square, but dances are confined to a central 1-*ken* space. A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (1).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
 == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja|島後神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-Kaigo Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Oki-Dozen Kagura}} * [http://stream.izm.ed.jp/izumi/kagura15.html Dozen Kagura (Shimane Prefectural Ancient Izumo Museum)] * [http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/kagura/kagura.html Dozen Kagura (Takuhi Shrine Official Site)] {{translated page|ja|隠岐島前神楽|version=92463223|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kagura]] }} [[ja:隠岐島前神楽]] [[Category:Shimane Islands]] [[Category:Festivals in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:Culture of the Oki Islands]] {{wikidata link|Q11659047}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

A "Gyokugai" (sacred canopy) adorned with five-colored [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|Shide]] hangs overhead, and large ceremonial staffs extend outward. The rear curtain serves as the entry point for performers, while musicians occupy three sides of the stage. == Music == Musicians surround the stage, with a "dō" (long drum) singer leading the {{ill|kamiuta|ja|神歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kamiuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式三番|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}. Percussion includes a long drum (1 player) and {{ill|shime-daiko|ja|締太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shime-daiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和太鼓|1=taiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q221769}} (3 players), accompanied by {{ill|tebyōshi|ja|手拍子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tebyōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (bronze clappers) played by 4 people. The unique alternation of 4/4 and 3/4 rhythms is rare in Japanese traditional music but common in Korean traditions, highlighting possible cultural connections. == Structure == The repertoire includes "Mai" (ritual dances) and "Noh" (masked performances). Ritual dances purify and invite deities, while Noh performances, influenced by {{ill|Izumo mythology|ja|出雲神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本神話|1=Japanese mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q814158}}, narrate mythological tales. Unique features include (1) dances for funerals, no longer performed, and (2) the use of lizard-like instead of serpent-like depictions for the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|八岐大蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamata no Orochi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマタノオロチ|1=Yamata no Orochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1054437}}. == Gallery == Below are images of Oki-Dozen Kagura at Yurahime Shrine. File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (2).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (3).jpg|
File:Oki-Dozen Kagura, Yurahime Shrine, Nishinoshima (4).jpg|
